message:
test(ft-xbnl0.2.4): large-body POST roundtrip contract (tick 327)

Added `distributed_http_client_post_large_body_roundtrips` — pins that
a 128 KiB POST body round-trips intact, covering the write-loop path
that small-body and empty-body tests don't exercise.

Why this matters: small-body (tick 316, 11 bytes) and empty-body (tick
326, 0 bytes) both fit in a single kernel write() syscall. A regression
that only surfaced for payloads crossing the kernel send-buffer
boundary — dropping bytes past the first EWOULDBLOCK, failing to await
between partial writes, or truncating the final chunk — would not
fire on either of those tests.

Production bodies routinely hit this size: captured pane scrollback,
detection events with rich context, and rollup event-batch forwarding
all push multi-KB bodies through the distributed HTTP client.

Body shape: 128 KiB total with sentinel headers/trailers (`RMS:` + `:END`)
so corruption at either end of the stream is caught explicitly, not
just via a bulk byte-equality assertion. Server read loop accumulates
chunks (read may return partial data at this size) and verifies:
1. Body length matches exactly.
2. Head and tail sentinels are preserved.
3. Full body bytes match the sent payload byte-for-byte.

Test result: ok. 1 passed; 0 failed
Compile time: 1m 24s cold.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
